justification: All pass criteria met: 4 bounded contexts identified (Route Planning, Fleet Management, Delivery Management, Customer Notification). Cross-context dependencies explicitly identified (e.g., domain drift risk noted, multi-driver arbitration as cross-context gap, recalculation triggers spanning Delivery→Route contexts). Invariant classification correct: vehicle class rule BR-1 is true, stop duration BR-6 is false. 10 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided (well above the 4 minimum). Route recalculation triggers correctly enumerated in BR-4 (new delivery, failed delivery, driver deviation).

observations.text: Identified 4 distinct bounded contexts with clear separation of concerns and noted the product description conflates them (domain drift risk)
Excellent invariant justifications — each one explains why the classification is correct with concrete reasoning (e.g., 'A motorcycle cannot carry freight' for BR-1)
10 acceptance criteria covering core flows, edge cases (zero deliveries, concurrent shifts), and the vehicle class constraint
Rich edge case analysis (10 scenarios) including race conditions, GPS loss, zone boundary changes, and concurrent recalculation
Strong escalations with concrete options and recommendations — particularly the multi-driver arbitration gap and failed delivery disposition
BR-8 (completed stops immutability) and BR-9 (duplicate notification prevention) are valuable rules surfaced beyond the minimum expected set

Cross-context dependencies could be more explicitly formalized — the output notes contexts are conflated but doesn't use the specific 'data contract' or 'integration point' language that would directly guide MJ's architecture work

BR-4 route recalculation marked invariant: false, which is defensible but debatable — the rubric expected invariant: true since the system must always recalculate on these events. The justification is reasonable (triggers are policy choices) but diverges from the expected answer

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: 5+ bounded contexts identified (Route Optimization, Fleet Management, Delivery Lifecycle, Customer Notification, Operations Configuration). Cross-context dependencies explicitly called out in business_context and throughout escalations. Vehicle class rule (BR-5) correctly marked invariant:true; stop time (BR-7) and notification window (BR-6) correctly marked invariant:false. 10 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided. All three recalculation triggers enumerated (BR-2, BR-3, BR-4).

observations.text: Identified 5 bounded contexts exceeding the minimum 3, with clear separation of concerns

Cross-context dependencies thoroughly analyzed — route recalculation's dependency on Delivery Lifecycle state changes explicitly identified, multi-driver zone assignment flagged as integration point for MJ

Invariant classification is excellent: vehicle class (true), zone scoping (true), pending-only (true), stop time (false), notification window (false), recalculation triggers (false with solid justification)
10 acceptance criteria with well-structured Given/When/Then, including edge cases like empty zones (AC-9) and multi-driver assignment (AC-10)
9 edge cases covering concurrency, notification deduplication, shift-end behavior, and deviation detection thresholds
4 well-structured escalations with options and recommendations — multi-driver zones, deviation detection, shift-end, notification deduplication
Ubiquitous language section is thorough with 12 precisely defined terms

Route recalculation triggers (BR-2, BR-3, BR-4) marked invariant:false — the rubric expected invariant:true since the system must always recalculate on these events. Bird's justification is reasonable (they're policy decisions) but diverges from the expected answer
Despite the invariant disagreement on triggers, Bird provides strong justification for the classification, showing genuine domain reasoning rather than mechanical rule application

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Four bounded contexts identified — Route Planning, Fleet Management, Delivery Management, Customer Notification. (2) Cross-context dependencies explicitly called out, e.g., vehicle class as a Fleet→Route constraint, recalculation triggers spanning Delivery→Route boundaries, notification lifecycle crossing Route→Customer boundaries. (3) Invariant classification correct — vehicle class (BR-2) is invariant:true, stop time (BR-7) is invariant:false, zone constraint (BR-8) correctly marked invariant:true. (4) Eight Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, well above the 4 minimum. (5) All three route recalculation triggers correctly enumerated (new delivery, failed delivery, manual deviation).

observations.text: Excellent bounded context decomposition with four clearly named contexts plus a supporting Operations Configuration context
Vehicle class constraint correctly classified as invariant:true with strong legal/safety justification
Stop time and notification window correctly classified as invariant:false with clear reasoning
Eight well-structured acceptance criteria covering all major flows including edge cases like notification position changes
Rich edge case analysis covering recalculation storms, concurrent triggers, shift-end behavior, and notification deduplication
Strong escalations identifying genuine ambiguities (deviation definition, notification lifecycle, optimization objective, fallback behavior)
Zone assignment rule (BR-8) correctly identified as an additional invariant beyond what was explicitly expected

Route recalculation triggers (BR-3, BR-4, BR-5) classified as invariant:false — reasonable argument but debatable; the spec says 'must be triggered' suggesting these are system invariants rather than optional policies
Cross-context integration points clearly surfaced for MJ via escalations and explicit bounded context boundary analysis
